title: 'Can’t Buy Me Love': Materialism and Marriage
gist: Monitoring the Future data show teen materialism surging and converging by gender since 1976, and the author argues -- against the popular framing of materialism as a rational cost-of-living response -- that it actively undermines marriage by turning shared financial milestones like homeownership into individual prerequisites, shifting marriage from a 'cornerstone' to a 'capstone' of adult life.

insights.claim: Monitoring the Future Study data analyzed by Grant Bailey show the share of high school seniors who say having 'lots of money' is important in life rose from 55% of boys and 36% of girls in 1976 to 74% of boys and 75% of girls in 2024 -- a 19-point increase for boys and a 39-point increase for girls, meaning girls' materialism has grown more than twice as fast and has now converged with boys'.
insights.speaker: Jason S. Carroll
insights.so_what: Materialism as a stated life value has become gender-neutral among teens over 50 years -- a leading indicator that financially-driven delay of marriage/family formation will not be a male- or female-specific phenomenon going forward.

themes.theme: Why materialism hurts marriage, not helps it

insights.claim: The author argues, against social-media commentators who called rising teen materialism a 'rational reaction' or 'survival assessment' to the cost of living, that materialism does not solve financial problems but makes them worse.
insights.speaker: Jason S. Carroll
insights.so_what: Rejects the popular cost-of-living defense of rising materialism -- implies interventions aimed at family formation should target attitudes toward money, not just affordability, as a lever.
insights.quote: Materialism doesn't solve financial problems; it makes them worse.

insights.claim: Brad Wilcox's 'Midas Mindset' concept -- the idea that fulfillment comes from career, money, and freedom from family obligations -- is cited as devaluing love, marriage, and children as this mindset spreads.
insights.speaker: Jason S. Carroll
insights.so_what: Names a specific cultural mechanism (career/freedom prioritization) that the author treats as a leading cause of family-formation decline, distinct from pure affordability constraints.
insights.locator: As Brad Wilcox has pointed out

insights.claim: Materialistic individuals are defined by three traits: prioritizing money and possessions above relationships, believing material goods are essential to happiness, and judging their own and others' success by material assets.
insights.speaker: Jason S. Carroll
insights.so_what: Gives a testable, three-part definition of materialism that could be used to screen or segment populations in family-formation or consumer-behavior research.
insights.locator: Researchers have identified three core traits

themes.theme: Marriage shifting from cornerstone to capstone

insights.claim: In a study of young adults' marriage readiness, 91% said financial independence from parents was necessary before marriage and 88% said they needed to no longer be living in their parents' house first.
insights.speaker: Jason S. Carroll
insights.so_what: Quantifies just how load-bearing financial-independence criteria have become as gatekeepers to marriage -- a concrete input for modeling the marriage-age distribution against housing and labor-market conditions.
insights.quote: 91% of young adults said that financial independence from parents was necessary for marriage readiness
insights.locator: At the top of the list

insights.claim: About half of surveyed young adults said they needed to be entirely finished with their education and settled into a long-term career before feeling ready to marry, and about 1 in 4 said they needed to have purchased a house first.
insights.speaker: Jason S. Carroll
insights.so_what: Shows young adults have added career-completion and homeownership as pre-marriage milestones that previous generations achieved jointly after marriage -- a compounding delay mechanism.
insights.locator: But the list didn't stop there

insights.claim: The author argues society is converting financial milestones couples used to achieve together into prerequisites individuals must accomplish alone before marrying, turning marriage from a 'cornerstone' of adult life-building into a 'capstone' pursued only after life is already built.
insights.speaker: Jason S. Carroll
insights.so_what: The cornerstone-to-capstone reframe is a compact mental model for why rising individual financial bars, not just aggregate cost of living, mechanically delay median marriage age.
insights.quote: we are taking the financial steps that couples used to achieve together and turning them into milestones individuals must accomplish on their own
insights.locator: As a culture, we are taking the financial steps

insights.claim: AEI analysis by Scott Winship shows married young adults (ages 25-34) are far more likely to be homeowners than single young adults as of 2025 -- 59% versus 15% -- and concludes married couples have always been in a better financial position to afford a home than singles.
insights.speaker: Jason S. Carroll
entities: Center on Opportunity and Social Mobility
insights.so_what: A roughly 4x homeownership gap between married and single young adults suggests marriage timing and housing-market access are tightly linked, relevant to both housing-demand forecasting and family-policy design.
insights.quote: Simply put, married couples are in a better position than single young adults to afford a home and always have been.
insights.locator: As new analyses from Scott Winship

insights.claim: Given rising materialism among today's teens, the author predicts an ever-longer list of financial reasons young adults will cite for not being ready to marry.
insights.speaker: Jason S. Carroll
insights.so_what: Implies the marriage-readiness checklist will keep expanding rather than stabilizing, a headwind to watch in future marriage-rate and household-formation data.
insights.locator: with materialism surging among today's teens
